data.name: Dayne St. Clair
data.description: A gentle giant off the pitch, St Clair is a different character on it and at 1.90m (6ft 3in), he is an intimidating sight for attackers. Armed with quick reflexes and strong hands, he became Minnesota United’s second Wonderwall during his time at the club – the first being the standing-room only section at their ground. After being named the MLS goalkeeper of the year in 2025, he said: “I almost feel like there’s a wall behind me and the wall on the line that means no ball’s going to go in.” The Wonderwall had a Bon Jovi-based tribute song for him, too: “Whoa he’s got great hair / whoa-oh, Dayne St Clair / with his hands, he’ll save it I swear / woah-oh, Dayne St Clair.” He moved to Inter Miami for the 2026 season, becoming teammates with Lionel Messi. “Any time that guy's on your team, the expectation is to win and to lift trophies,” he said. “To have those expectations and that pressure is a privilege.”

data.name: Alistair Johnston
data.description: Born into a football-loving family, Johnston used to wake up at 4.30am on Saturday mornings in British Columbia to watch English Premier League matches live, and has never lost that love for the game. Enthusiastic, hard-tackling and full of natural leadership and passion, he is loved by Celtic fans for understanding what it means to represent their club, ever since the day he arrived back in December 2022. He told the Guardian last year: “I see what the club means to people. I understand that if I’m a fan of a club like this, what would I like to see from my players? I’m honest with my work, defensively love a tackle, love being physical. I’m a bit football mad. I love the city. [It’s] the biggest fish bowl in all of Europe. Everyone knows who you are. You don’t know if they love you or hate you but you know they know who you are. I’m not famous outside of maybe a 30-mile radius of Glasgow city centre … but what a radius.” An excellent passer and relentless one-on-one defender, he can play in multiple positions but has all of the tools required of a modern full-back.

data.name: Alfie Jones
data.description: A casual locker room chat with the Canada winger Liam Millar when the pair were at Hull together in the 2024-25 season proved career-changing for Jones. The Bristol-born defender mentioned to Millar that he had a Canadian grandmother, so his clubmate pointed out the possibilities. What followed was a whirlwind period for Jones, who went through the process to earn citizenship and made his Canada debut against Venezuela in November last year. A hulking 1.90m (6ft 3in) centre-back known for aggression, ball-playing skills and aerial prowess, he was named Hull’s player of the year in 2023 – chosen by the then manager, Liam Rosenior – before joining Middlesbrough last year. Jones had an injury-disrupted season, but regained sharpness in time to take his place in this summer’s squad. “I feel there's a connection there, it's in my bloodline being linked to Canada, and I'm a very emotional guy,” he said last year. “So, the thought of putting on the Canadian shirt and just doing the best I can to represent the country is what drives me.”

data.name: Luc de Fougerolles
data.description: At one stage last year De Fougerolles, whose dad was born in Montreal, had more appearances for Canada than he did as a club professional – evidence of what a big impression the youngster has made in the international setup. Tasked to step in as Canada dealt with a rash of injuries in 2023, he immediately looked at home, showing positional awareness and composure on the ball. Born in London, De Fougerolles joined Fulham’s academy aged eight, and signed his first professional deal in 2023, winning scholar of the year. He joined the Belgian Pro League side Dender that July to continue his development. “Meeting new teammates, living in a different country, everything about it has been completely different for me,” he said last year. “I’ve only ever known Fulham. To change that around, to go and live alone in Belgium has been tough, but my teammates have made it easy and it’s been a great group. I’ve really enjoyed my time here.” He was named Canada’s young player of the year for 2025.

data.name: Joel Waterman
data.description: Waterman made history in 2020 as the first player from the new Canadian Premier League to transfer to an MLS club, when he joined CF Montréal from Cavalry FC. That proved a shrewd decision for Waterman, who had been playing at the Canadian university level in 2018 before joining Cavalry. He has grown into an important player for Montréal, becoming one of the club’s captains during his last few seasons. A former midfielder, he is comfortable on the ball but also aggressive defensively, which has helped him become a regular in the national setup and for his new club, Chicago Fire. Waterman is also part of a big sporting family: he is married to Carli Grosso, sister of the Canada midfielder Julia Grosso, who is engaged to the Houston Astros baseball star Jeremy Peña.

data.name: Mathieu Choinière
data.description: It’s not often that a player gets to live out the dream of becoming a star player with their hometown team, but Mathieu Choinière experienced that with CF Montréal, earning two MLS All-Star game places during his seven seasons at the club. His consistency didn’t go unnoticed. He made the leap into European football with Grasshoppers in 2024, then moved back to MLS with the Swiss side’s parent club, Los Angeles FC, last year. LAFC’s co-president, John Thorrington, praised his “experience, intelligence, calm authority and competitive edge … we’re also excited about the opportunity for Mathieu to represent his country on the biggest stage and proud to support him in that ambition.” In an LA team filled with star names, Choinière fits in perfectly with his work ethic and his versatility, having played as a wing-back and winger for Montréal before settling into central midfield. He is also one of Canada’s best set-piece takers.

data.name: Stephen Eustáquio
data.description: While others can claim to be the face of this Canadian team, Eustáquio is undoubtedly the heartbeat. A passionate leader, some of his best performances for club and country have come on the biggest stages, as he showed with the Portuguese giants Porto, where he won multiple trophies. A tireless box-to-box runner, he brings huge versatility, equally adept as a box-crasher, a ball-progresser, a deep-lying defensive blocker, and even a centre-back – a coach's dream. Born in Ontario, not far from the US border, when he was seven Eustáquio moved with his Portuguese parents back to Portugal, where he began his journey into professional football. He moved from Porto to LAFC on loan in February this year, adding strength to their star-studded team. His Canada teammate Richie Laryea says Eustáquio’s importance to the national side cannot be overstated: “He does a lot of the dirty work that people don't really recognise.”

data.name: Ismaël Koné
data.description: At his best, Koné is a sight to behold, gliding across midfield, finding space and leaving defenders in his wake. He has the ability to light up games, as he showed in a 2024 friendly between Canada and France when he stood out despite being surrounded by World Cup winners. Born in Côte d’Ivoire, he moved with his mother to Montreal aged seven and as a teenager made his name with the city’s MLS side. Koné was fast-tracked from there to Europe, first to Watford in 2022, then Marseille two years later. He joined Sassuolo last year, initially on loan before the move was made permanent in February. He may be softly spoken but Koné has a fierce competitive spirit and is determined to improve his consistency. Last year, Jesse Marsch labelled him a work in progress. “I’ve made it very clear to him that we all believe in his talent,” said Canada's coach, “but we need to refine him as a soccer player, on the discipline and concentration of what he does on the pitch … I’m definitely not trying to limit his talents and his creativity, but I’m trying to add more tactical discipline.”

data.name: Cyle Larin
data.description: Whatever happens this summer, Larin will forever be a cult hero in Canada for his displays during the 2022 qualifying cycle when he scored 13 goals in 16 games, helping Canada reach their first World Cup since 1986. Known for his smart movement in the box and first-time finishing, he can be a real handful as he showed during a brief spell at Real Valladolid, where fans coined the term “Larinisimo”. Jesse Marsch said in March that Larin’s loan to Southampton was perfect timing. “It’s exactly what he needed," the Canada coach said. "He's playing outstanding, he’s made a huge impact and will come into the World Cup in form, which is exactly what we need. I couldn’t be more excited for Cyle right now." When he signed for the Saints, Larin, who had already played in the US, Canada, Turkey, Belgium, Spain and the Netherlands, told their website that playing in England “has always been an itch. The style of play, the way of football in England is something I’ve always wanted to live and breathe.”

data.name: Jonathan David
data.description: “He's the smartest player I've ever coached,” says Jesse Marsch. David really is a cerebral striker, a two-footed finisher with the workrate of a midfielder. He has undergone quite the journey from timid youngster playing in the Canadian capital, Ottawa, to becoming a global star with Juventus. It was his bold gamble as a teenager to sign with the Belgian side Gent instead of staying in Canada that accelerated his road to the top. David then moved to the Ligue 1 side Lille and immediately played his part in their surprise 2020-21 French title win, before blossoming into one of the best strikers in Europe across his half-decade in France. Having been heavily linked with a move to England, David told the Guardian last year: “For me, watching the Premier League, the biggest difference it’s just the intensity. The game is always at a high-end tempo, always 100% back and forth for 90 minutes.” Known as the Iceman for his cool, unflappable nature, David sees himself as “someone that’s always calm. As a striker, someone who’s relied on to score goals, I think that quality comes in handy.” After a slow start in Turin, the striker earned more game time under Luciano Spalletti as the season unfolded.

data.name: Liam Millar
data.description: Raised in Liverpool’s academy, Millar has gone on to build a solid career, with loans at Kilmarnock and Charlton followed by a breakthrough period in Switzerland with Basel. He moved back to England with Preston on loan in 2023, then signed for Hull on a permanent deal in August 2024. Millar sustained an anterior cruciate ligament injury that October, needing surgery and a lengthy recovery period, but regained his strength and form thanks to “working my backside off for 12 months”. Millar is from a football family: his grandad Malcolm played for Sunderland, while his dad, Alan, turned out for Charlton. Alan went on to work in the TV and film industry as an electrician on Game of Thrones, Peaky Blinders and X-Men, while his mum, Jo-Ann, worked as a make-up artist on movies such as Resident Evil, Suicide Squad and Dune.

data.name: Tani Oluwaseyi
data.description: A real team player, Oluwaseyi has a great engine, always running as hard to help defend as he does when attacking. A late bloomer who didn’t become an MLS regular until he was 23, his work ethic and knack for finding himself open in the box at perfect moments led to the coining of the term “Tani Time” at Minnesota United. His performances there earned a big move last year to Villarreal, where he continued to develop his all-round game, saying: “Playing and training in Spain has already made me a smarter player.” Born in Nigeria, Oluwaseyi moved with his family to Mississauga, Canada at the age of 10. He spoke last year about what it would mean to represent Canada at a World Cup: “To be honest, I think until it happens, until we hear the [national] anthem, it won’t feel real. But I’m really excited for it.”

data.name: Derek Cornelius
data.description: A striker until his teenage years, Cornelius still has some solid attacking instincts, as shown when he scored a fine long-range free-kick in a 1-0 friendly win against Wales last year. “I was working on it in training this week,” he said. “I felt confident and was in a good space, and I got good contact and it went in.” Typically, though, he’s known as an old-school centre-back, not shy to throw himself in front of, or at the ball in order to protect the box. With a CV that includes stops in Serbia, Germany, Canada, Greece, Sweden, France and now Scotland, Cornelius brings plenty of experience, but has also had a difficult season with Rangers, his loan spell undermined by injury problems. He will be hoping to prove the doubters wrong alongside Moïse Bombito in the heart of Canada’s defence.

data.name: Jacob Shaffelburg
data.description: It’s not often that a player’s hair earns its own nickname, but Shaffelburg’s golden locks were known as the “Tennessee Waterslide” at his previous club Nashville SC, a nod to how the mullet flows behind him when he’s sprinting. His game may not be as flashy as his hair, but he is still an effective, tireless runner who knows how to strike the ball with power and technique. Born in Nova Scotia, Shaffelburg has form for rising to the moment on the big stage, having shone for Canada at the 2024 Copa América. He moved to Hollywood with LAFC last December, and arrives at this World Cup in a positive mood. Shaffelburg says playing under Jesse Marsch suits him: “The style of play just kind of fits me. He tells me just to get in and run behind, and that's all I want to do in general. It's right up my alley.”

data.name: Moïse Bombito
data.description: At the last World Cup Bombito was 22, watching Canada on TV from his college campus. Now he is Canada’s rock at the back, the key who allows Jesse Marsch to play his high-pressing system. A late bloomer, Bombito was a winger until a coach suggested his pace, technique and physicality would make him a top central defender. Thanks to that positional shift he had a rapid rise to MLS, earning a first Canada call-up in 2023. His displays at the 2024 Copa América earned him a big move to Ligue 1 with Nice. “I chose Nice because of the human factor,” Bombito said last year. “You know, many top clubs look for a player who performs well. But they don't care about their mentality, their background, or what their family does. But I felt like the organisation here focused on who I am … This has all happened very quickly for me, I started playing at professional level when I was 23 … this club knows it will take time for me to adapt. I am very proud of my choice.” Bombito, who wears the No 64 in tribute to the Montreal-area bus he used to take to school, is currently recovering from a tibia fracture sustained in October. Marsch, who says the centre-back “can be our best player and is a future captain of this team”, is hoping he can win his fitness race to play at the World Cup.

data.name: Maxime Crépeau
data.description: Less than a month out from what was supposed to be his first World Cup in 2022, Crépeau suffered an unfortunate leg injury, one that ruled him out of the tournament and kept him out of action for almost a year. If you were to ask him if he’d change a thing, he’d say no. The injury came on a game-saving stop in the 2022 MLS Cup final between Crépeau’s LAFC and Philadelphia Union, which LAFC went on to win. "You don't cut corners [as a keeper],” he said later. “You have to go all out, and I went all out. This is how I play." A smaller keeper at 1.81m (5ft 11in) tall, Crépeau certainly plays much bigger than his height, as he is unafraid to protect his box and his net. With his quick feet and strong reflexes, he loves playing in the biggest games, as he showed when he helped Canada reach the semi-finals of the 2024 Copa América. He speaks French, English and – due to his wife’s family being from Chile – Spanish, and his little daughter likes to mix it up. “Sometimes my daughter will start a sentence in French, will finish it in Spanish, and in the middle she'll put one or two words in English in there.”

data.name: Tajon Buchanan
data.description: In a world where modern wingers can sometimes look robotic, Buchanan is old-school: an unpredictable attacker who loves to take on defenders using an arsenal of body feints, stepovers and flicks. A late bloomer who emerged from the NCAA college system, he made his name with the MLS side New England Revolution from 2019. Buchanan developed the defensive side of his game so well that he played as a full-back for Club Brugge and a wingback at Inter in Serie A. He has always been most comfortable on the wing, though, and a move to Spain with Villarreal last year allowed him to return to that position, to the benefit of club and country. A quiet, serious character, Buchanan has had to show real resilience to progress in the game. His father died when he was just seven, and he left home at 15 to move to Colorado with a friend’s family. “I’m more introverted. It all comes naturally to me,” he told the Athletic. “Since I was a teenager, I’ve been away from home, alone, trying to chase my dream.”

data.name: Owen Goodman
data.description: There’s no doubting Goodman’s commitment to Canada. The young keeper, who was born in London but lived in Ontario from the age of five to 13, hired an immigration lawyer last year to help him get a Canadian passport, with one eye on the World Cup. The persistence paid off as he passed his citizenship test, and earned a senior Canada call-up. “Once I’ve got my mind on one thing, there’s nothing that’s going to stop me. No one’s going to get in my way,” he told the Athletic, having previously played for England at youth level and turned down an approach from Nigeria while he was waiting for the application to be processed. When it was, he said: “I had shivers go down my spine a little bit … I was waiting so long you start to lose a bit of hope. I wanted to jump for joy.” A talented shot stopper with a big frame, Goodman is an exciting prospect, and has developed on loan from Palace at AFC Wimbledon, Huddersfield and most recently Barnsley, with the next step likely to be a Championship loan.

data.name: Alphonso Davies
data.description: When Davies moves his feet, he flies – and it certainly felt like he had wings when he leapt to head home Canada’s first-ever World Cup goal in 2022. “When I ran to the corner flag, I looked to my left and I just saw all my teammates running towards me. When they all came around me, a lot of players were grabbing my jersey, pushing me, shoving me. I had Liam Millar headbutt me at one point, he was so excited. We both looked at each other and started laughing." It made a big impact in the stands too: his parents, who had fled the civil war in Liberia to Ghana, where Davies was born in a refugee camp, before finding safe haven in Canada, were in the stadium watching. “They told me how proud they are of me and when I scored the goal, my mother teared up a bit. My parents are people of few words, they don't really say too much. But it really showed on their faces.” Back in 2018, aged 17, he gave a speech about his background to Fifa delegates in Moscow as part of the Canadian bid to host this World Cup. Davies then joined Bayern Munich from Vancouver Whitecaps, blossoming into one of the world’s best left-backs, and winning everything available to him along the way.

data.name: Ali Ahmed
data.description: As a youngster, Ahmed failed trials at Enfield Town, Cheshunt and Tooting & Mitcham. Now he’s part of a World Cup squad, and brings huge versatility to Canada’s mix. He broke through at Vancouver Whitecaps during three eye-catching years in MLS where he featured at full-back, wingback, central midfielder and attacking midfielder before specialising as a winger – a move which helped him blossom into one of the league’s best talents, and earn a transfer to Norwich in January. Ahmed is an effective if unorthodox dribbler who likes to use body feints – a nod to his strong love for basketball. He says that life in England has added grit to his game: “Before I came into the Championship, everyone I spoke to talked about the intensity and the physicality of it and it has lived up to every single word. I think that's good for me – it's an area I want to improve.” Reflecting on his return to England, five years after that series of trials at non-league clubs, he said: “Football is crazy and every player has their own journey to get to where they are. But if you told me then that I would be back in five years playing in the Championship, I don't know if I would have believed it.”

data.name: Jonathan Osorio
data.description: A rare one-club man in the modern game, Osorio has been with his hometown team, Toronto FC, for 14 seasons, winning an MLS Cup, a Supporters’ Shield and three Canadian Championships. A rambunctious box-crashing midfielder in his younger days, he has scored some big goals for club and country, such as his cheeky back-heel against Tigres during the run to the 2018 Concacaf Champions Cup final, and his crucial goal for Canada against Mexico at the Estadio Azteca during 2022 World Cup qualifying. One of the true faces of Canadian soccer and closing in on 100 caps, he brings a wealth of knowhow to this summer’s squad, with Jesse Marsch praising him pre-tournament for his “intensity, covering the ground, being hard to play against, being a dictator of the rhythm of the game … Oso’s experience, his stability, his mentality, his personality – he brings so much to the group.”

data.name: Richie Laryea
data.description: It’s a cliche but it fits Laryea perfectly: he leaves everything on the pitch, every time he plays. It doesn’t matter if he’s making an overlapping run forward, getting stuck into a tackle or stepping up for a teammate, Laryea does it at 100%, playing on the edge without crossing the line. “When it gets hostile, I get even better,” he says. “I get in and around the line, then I stay there and don’t go past it.” A serious attacking threat, the Toronto FC man specialises in low crosses and winning fouls, but is also a capable one-v-one defender who can play at left- and right-back. He was arguably Canada’s best player in 2025 and his form has allowed Alphonso Davies to operate further forward for the national team. Laryea had an unsuccessful spell at Nottingham Forest, signing in 2022 but returning to Toronto after six months, initially on loan. “No negatives. A lot of positives,” he said about the Forest move. “I think it's helped me a lot to grow as a player and a person, especially off the field. Other people might have different perspectives … maybe people thought I was unhappy or miserable, but I think it was the opposite."

data.name: Niko Sigur
data.description: Most players know their preferred position but Sigur, who has played as a defensive midfielder and full-back for club and country, is keeping his options open. “If the coach trusts you in those [different] positions,” he said, “then you have a bit of an advantage over other players.” Sigur, who holds dual citizenship of Canada and Croatia, should be a very handy Swiss Army knife of a player for Jesse Marsch this summer. He was heavily linked with Celtic and Wolves in the January window, and could be the focus of more speculation once the World Cup is over. Sigur told the Athletic last year: “I think it’s in Hajduk’s best interest to sell me at some point. It’s a matter of time. And I think I’m ready for it. I want it to make sense. I don’t want to go to a huge club and go on loan or sit on the bench. I want to play.”

data.name: Promise David
data.description: No relation to Jonathan, Promise David has become a fan favourite thanks to his smile, personality and 1.96m (6ft 5in) frame. An injury scare put his involvement this summer in doubt, but he recovered to earn his place in Marsch’s squad, and deserves it for his drive and commitment. As one unfortunate Antwerp defender learned last season, you can try to literally rip the shirt off David’s back and that still won’t stop him from scoring. Despite his size, David is far from a one-dimensional centre-forward, often earning praise for his deft feet and soft touch, which has allowed him to rack up an impressive and varied goal catalogue. Since arriving in Belgium from the Estonian league he has developed his all-round game, without losing his instinctive scoring touch, and helped lead Union Saint-Gilloise to their first league title in 90 years. He has been linked with several Premier League clubs, but his current side will want big money.

data.name: Nathan Saliba
data.description: Saliba’s progress as a youngster with his hometown side, CF Montréal, was rapid. Within 12 months of his club breakthrough he made his Canada debut, was named one of the best under-22s in MLS, and then moved to the Belgian side Anderlecht last year for an undisclosed fee, with Montréal retaining a sell-on clause. “His qualities are insane,” his then teammate Ismaël Koné said at the time. “He’s one of those players that I can’t wait to flourish. Because he has so much to show, a lot of gifts to give. And I just can’t wait for you guys to see where he’s going to end up, and how big he’s going to be for the country.” There was plenty of praise, too, from Jesse Marsch, with the Canada coach saying: “He has a really good combination of intelligence, athleticism, technical ability, tactical awareness, responsibility, commitment to the game, a good eye for good attacking plays … we really believe he can be an important player for us.” Saliba’s parents are both Haitian and he speaks three languages: French, English and Haitian Creole.

data.name: Jayden Nelson
data.description: Nelson has been through so much to get to this point. At 18 months old he was diagnosed with a rare form of cancer, which his doctors thought would not be survivable. Sixteen years later his performances at youth level had earned him “next Alphonso Davies” billing, only for his career to stall. After struggling to break through at his boyhood club, Toronto, he headed to the Norwegian side Rosenborg. Nelson finally started to show glimpses of his best when he returned to Canada with Vancouver, including an explosive debut: a goal and three assists in a 4-1 win over Portland Timbers. As he celebrated scoring, he mouthed the words: “I’m here.” Nelson signed for Austin this year and opened up about how his childhood trauma had shaped him. “I could have died, but I defied the odds, and I want to tell my story so other kids can be inspired to do the same. You can come out of dark situations brighter.” Nelson also explained why he has grown his hair out. “For me, it's a symbol of strength. When I had chemotherapy I had no hair … it makes me feel confident. It reminds me there are more important things in life.”
